coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a novel ratiometric nanoprobe (CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+) that integrates fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and Eu3+ on LZIF-8 via a one-pot stirring method. Tetracyclines (TCs) can create inner filter effect on CDs while simultaneously generating an antenna effect with Eu3+. The rapid and efficient adsorption capabilities of LZIF-8 enhance the probability of TCs interacting with the signal tags. Additionally, energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ linked by LZIF-8, sensitizes the fluorescence changes, resulting in signal amplification. The nanoprobe exhibits reverse response signals with high sensitivity (2.94–4.46 ng/mL), rapid response (within 10 s), and visible color change (blue to red). The practicability of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanoprobe was demonstrated in food and environmental samples, accurately discriminating four TCs through principal component analysis. Furthermore, the first portable nanofibers mat based solid-phase fluorescence sensing platform was fabricated by an in-situ growth method. Combined with a smartphone-assisted device, rapid on-site monitoring of TCs can be achieved.

head.ce:title: An ingenious integrated metal-organic frameworks-based ratiometric sensing platform for efficient, sensitive and real-time detection of tetracyclines

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a novel ratiometric nanoprobe (CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+) that integrates fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and Eu3+ on LZIF-8 via a one-pot stirring method. Tetracyclines (TCs) can create inner filter effect on CDs while simultaneously generating an antenna effect with Eu3+. The rapid and efficient adsorption capabilities of LZIF-8 enhance the probability of TCs interacting with the signal tags. Additionally, energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ linked by LZIF-8, sensitizes the fluorescence changes, resulting in signal amplification. The nanoprobe exhibits reverse response signals with high sensitivity (2.94–4.46 ng/mL), rapid response (within 10 s), and visible color change (blue to red). The practicability of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanoprobe was demonstrated in food and environmental samples, accurately discriminating four TCs through principal component analysis. Furthermore, the first portable nanofibers mat based solid-phase fluorescence sensing platform was fabricated by an in-situ growth method. Combined with a smartphone-assisted device, rapid on-site monitoring of TCs can be achieved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are a versatile type of antibiotic that are cost-effective and widely used in medical and agricultural settings to prevent and treat intestinal infections. They are also commonly added to animal feed to promote the growth of livestock (Li, Zhu, et al., 2019). As the second most commonly used antibiotic globally and the most frequently used in China, the abuse of TCs has resulted in their persistent accumulation in the environment, leading to undesirable drug resistance (Chang et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023; Xin et al., 2022). Moreover, elevated levels of antibiotics in animal products, such as meat, eggs, and milk, can pose health risks to humans, including hepatotoxicity, gastrointestinal disorders, and allergic reactions (Wei et al., 2019). In response to concerns about public health and environmental safety, many countries and organizations have classified TCs as a top-priority pollutant and have implemented strict regulations regarding their presence in animal-based food products (Liang et al., 2022). Therefore, it is essential to develop effective and convenient detection methods for monitoring and controlling the presence of TC residues in environment and food samples.

Different techniques have been investigated for identifying TCs, including liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Thomas et al., 2022), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Qiu et al., 2020), enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) (Wang, Liu et al., 2022), and electrochemical analysis (Huang et al., 2023; Wang, Zhangsun, et al., 2021). LC-MS and CE can detect TCs with high accuracy and specificity. However, the sample preparation process is labor-intensive and the required instruments are relatively expensive. ELISA exhibits strong specificity for TCs, but the detection time is relatively long, and the immunoassay may yield false positive test results. Electrochemical detection offers high sensitivity, but its repeatability is poor. Currently, fluorescence sensors are gaining popularity due to their superior accessibility and streamlined analytical process. Remarkably, the visual signal transduction of fluorescence is more suitable for on-site real-time monitoring.

As a new type of promising luminescent material, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant interest due to their good biocompatibility, green preparation process, and intriguing chemical and photophysical characteristics (Rong et al., 2024; Xia et al., 2019). Currently, various CDs have been developed for detecting TCs (Wang, Teng, et al., 2021; Wang, Zhang, et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2025; Li, Li, et al., 2024). The presence of diverse chemical groups on CDs enables effective binding with TCs, resulting in the suppression of the fluorescence signal. However, conventional CDs-based fluorescence sensors, depended solely on the fluorescence change of a single luminogen always cannot achieve the expected effect, because measurement accuracy can be significantly influenced by environmental conditions, instrument efficiency and excitation variables (Ehtesabi et al., 2019; Zhong et al., 2023). Additionally, the small particle size of CDs can lead to aggregation due to various interferences, resulting in fluorescence self-quenching and adversely impacting their sensing performance (Han et al., 2021; Thara et al., 2023; Wang, Li, et al., 2022). Therefore, the development of novel fluorescence sensor with high sensitivity and favorable stability will be a promising aspect for fulfilling the visualization analysis of TCs.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), an emerging class of porous materials formed through the self-assembly of organic ligands and metal ions or clusters, exhibit great prospects in sensing, separation, gas storage, and catalysis (Duan et al., 2024; Payam et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024). Owing to their adjustable pore size, ultrahigh surface area and excellent chemical stability, MOFs are particularly suitable for encapsulating nanoparticles, enzymes, and fluorescent dyes, etc. This encapsulation can create protective layers that enhance the durability of embedded components in various environments and prevent aggregating (Ahmad et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023; Ren, Yuan, et al., 2024). Furthermore, when functionalized with suitable groups, MOFs can effectively bind chromogenic fluorescent groups, forming dual-emission fluorescent sensing platforms. The inherent calibration effect of two luminogens can significantly enhance detection precision due to their robust resistance to interference.

Lanthanide ions, particularly Eu3+, are ideal candidates for fluorescence applications due to their significantly enhanced emission properties when bound to TCs, primarily through the antenna effect (Zhang, Wang, et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023). Due to the prohibited f-f transitions, Eu3+ is difficult to be excited in water, and due to the shielding effect of the outermost orbital electrons, Eu3+ is not sensitive to the environment change. Consequently, a suitable ligand acting as an antenna is the key to opening the fluorescence sensing. The β-diketone segment of TCs possesses a strong coordination ability with Eu3+, facilitating the efficient transfer of energy absorbed by the antenna ligands TCs from ultraviolet radiation to Eu3+, ultimately leading to fluorescence emission (Arkin et al., 2023; Wu, Chen, et al., 2023; Wu, Li, et al., 2023).

Inspired by these insights, an efficient dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was designed for the rapid detection of TCs using a straightforward self-assembly strategy involving L-histidine-modified ZIF-8 (LZIF-8) as an ingenious medium. Through the strong chelation between Eu3+ and citric acid grafted on the outer surface of LZIF-8, along with the effective encapsulation of blue fluorescent CDs under one-pot stirring, CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ was successfully obtained (Fig. 1a). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first fluorescence probe that integrates CDs and europium ions Eu3+ with MOFs as a bridging component. Upon the introduction of TCs, two key interactions occur: first, TCs induce an inner filter effect (IFE) with CDs, leading to fluorescence quenching at 421 nm; second, TCs coordinate with Eu3+ to generate an antenna effect (AE), resulting in the characteristic red fluorescence of Eu3+ at 617 nm. This efficient dual-channel strategy allows CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ to exhibit double and reverse response signals, along with a notable transition in fluorescent color from blue to red. Moreover, LZIF-8 demonstrates superior adsorption capabilities for TCs, achieving high sensitivity and visual detection of TCs. The practicality of CDs@MOFs-Eu3+ was evaluated in various water and food samples. Different TCs have different effects on the sensor. Based on this principle, the new sensor effectively distinguished among four TCs, with each antibiotic producing a distinct recognition pattern analyzed through Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Additionally, by in-situ growing the probe on a solid-phase nanofiber mat (Fig. 1b) and combining it with a black box and a smartphone, a Lab-on-Nanofibers device was established for the first time, allowing on-site and out-of-lab monitoring of TCs.

The targets tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC), along with HPLC-grade methanol and formic acid, were provided by Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co., Ltd. Analytical-grade 2-methylimidazole (2-Meim), L-histidine (L-His), sodium citrate (CitNa), europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), adenine, glucose, glycine, sucrose, N-[(tert-Butoxy)carbonyl]-D-tryptophan, l-lysine, magnesium chloride (MgCl2), calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), zinc acetate (Zn(AC)2), copper acetate (Cu(AC)2), ferric chloride (FeCl3), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and polyacrylonitrile (PAN) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. Zn(NO3)2 was purchased from Henan Huazhiye Technology Co., Ltd.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were captured by ZEISS Gemini 300 and JEOL JEM-F200, respectively. The chemical structure and functional groups of the prepared materials were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of LZIF-8 and N-CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ were obtained using a MAX-2500 powder X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) at diffraction angles ranging from 10o to 50o. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (US). Luminescence lifetime and quantum yield were measured with an Edinburgh FLS 1000 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (UK). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was carried out on Micromeritics ASAP 2460 (American). Thermogravimetric analysis was performed on Netzsch STA 449 F3 (German). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured using Edinburgh DS5 and Edinburgh FS5 spectrophotometer, respectively.

Preparation of CDs CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with citric acid and adenine as precursors (Wu, Chen, et al., 2023; Wu, Li, et al., 2023). In a nutshell, citric acid (8 mmol, 2.304 g) and adenine (4 mmol, 0.810 g) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. The resulting solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Afterward, the yellow solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to eliminate solid impurities. The filtrate was then purified using a 1000 Da dialysis membrane for 24 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for direct use in subsequent experiments without further processing.

Preparation of LZIF-8 The synthesis of LZIF-8 was developed from a previously reported method with slight modifications (Chen et al., 2023). Initially, 155 mg (1 mmol) L-His was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, 250 μL of triethylamine and 5.747 g of 2-Meim (70 mmol) in 20 mL of methanol were added to this solution and stirred vigorously for 15 min. Finally, 0.3788 g of Zn(NO3)2 (2 mmol) in 40 mL of methanol was added. After stirring at room temperature for 24 h, the resulting white solid was collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min, washed thrice with methanol to remove any unreacted reactants, and dried at 60 °C for further use.

Preparation of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ The CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ was synthesized using a one-pot stirring method. Solutions of LZIF-8 (1.0 mg/mL), Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (30 mmol/L), CitNa (20 mmol/L) were initially prepared in deionized water. Then, 10 mL of the LZIF-8 solution, 5 mL of the Eu(NO3)3·6H2O solution and 1 mL of the CitNa solution were mixed and stirred for 20 min. Through the cross-linking of CitNa with L-His and the chelation of CitNa with Eu3+, LZIF-8@Eu3+ composites were obtained. Following this, 2.5 mL of the above prepared CDs solution was added to the mixture and stirred for another 1.0 h. The resulting yellow precipitate was collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and the product was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water for subsequent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the nanofiber mat-based CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ based nanofiber mat was prepared using the in-situ growth method, with PAN nanofibers mat as the template. PAN nanofibers were prepared by electrospinning following our previous work with some modifications (Jian et al., 2019). 3.0 g of PAN (Mw = 85,000) was added to 20 mL of DMF and stirred overnight to obtain a clear and transparent solution. Subsequently, the PAN/DMF solution was transferred to the electrospinning apparatus and spun at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/h using a spinning jet sized 20 G. A high voltage of 20 kV was applied, and the distance between the collector and the spinneret was set at 15 cm. The temperature and relative humidity were kept at 25 °C and 45 %, respectively. After electrospinning for 4.0 h, a piece of PAN nanofibers mat with the appropriate thickness was obtained.

The PAN nanofibers mat was hydrolyzed in a 1.0 mol/L NaOH solution at 60 °C for 1.5 h, then rinsed with deionized water until the wash solution reached a neutral pH (Zhao et al., 2023). Next, the partially hydrolyzed and yellowish PAN mat was soaked in 20 mL of Zn(NO3)2 solution (69.7 mg, 0.368 mmol) in methanol, sonicated for 10 min and shaken for 2.0 h. Afterwards, 250 μL of triethylamine, 2.26 g (27.5 mmol) of 2-Meim in 20 mL of methanol and 0.609 g (3.93 mmol) L-His in 15 mL of water were added. The resulting mixture was stirred at 45 °C for 3.0 h to yield the LZIF-8 mat, which was further purified by washing with deionized water and dried in a vacuum at 60 °C.

Subsequently, the prepared mat was immersed in a mixed water solution of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (30 mM, 10 mL) and CitNa (20 mM, 2 mL) and subjected to oscillation for 1.0 h. Following this, 10 mL of CDs solution was added, as outlined in the Preparation of CDs section, and the oscillation was sustained for an additional 4.0 h. The mat was then retrieved and dried for subsequent detection.

The effectiveness of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ as a reliable tool for analyzing real samples was confirmed by testing various matrices, including food (chicken meat, egg, milk, and honey) and environmental water samples. The chicken meat, egg, milk and honey samples were obtained from a local supermarket in Zhengzhou, Henan Province, China. For the environmental water samples, the Jialu Lake water in Zhengzhou was collected in April 2024. Two tap water samples from a residential building and one reservoir water sample were collected in June 2024 in Pingdingshan, Henan Province, China. The water samples were placed in pre-cleaned 250 mL amber glass bottles and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

Solvent extraction was conducted to isolate the TCs residues in chicken meat, egg, and milk samples. Specifically, 1.0 g of the homogenized sample was weighed into 5.0 mL centrifuge tubes. Subsequently, 1 mL of acetonitrile was introduced into the tubes. The mixtures were vigorously mixed using a homogenizer for 30 s and then subjected to sonication for 10 min. After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C, the supernatant was filtered through a needle filter of 0.22 μm, and the resulting filtrate was diluted 20-fold with water for subsequent determination. In the case of honey samples, they were diluted 20-fold directly with water and filtered. Water samples were used directly without any further treatment.

To align with the actual application, matrix-matched spiked samples at different concentrations of TCs were utilized for the methodological evaluation. These samples were prepared by adding working standard solutions to the seven types of blank samples (tap water, lake water, reservoir water samples, and chicken meat, egg, milk, and honey samples), vortexed adequately, stored in the dark for 2 h, and then performed the above sample pretreatment process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for ratiometric fluorescence determination of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: The TCs stock solutions (1.0 mg/mL in methanol) were diluted to a series of working solutions with varying concentrations. Subsequently, 1.8 mL of these TCs working solutions or the matrix-matched spiked samples prepared as described in Section 2.4 were mixed with 200 μL of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ solution, resulting in a total volume of 2 mL in a tube. After incubating at 25 °C for 10 s, the fluorescence spectra were measured by fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FS5) with an excitation wavelength of 335 nm. The response of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ to potential interferents was also studied under the same conditions.

The morphologies of CDs, LZIF-8, CDs@LZIF-8 and CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ were examined using SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2a, the CDs exhibited a uniform size distribution, with an average size of approximately 2.5 nm. The TEM image of CDs@LZIF-8 in Fig. S1 reveals a homogeneous distribution of CDs within the internal structure of LZIF-8. In contrast to the regular diamond dodecahedron structure of LZIF-8 (Fig. 2b), the as-synthesized CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ displayed a distinct rough, near- spherical morphology, with an increased particle size of approximately 100 nm (Fig. 2c). The evident core-shell structure observed from the TEM image of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ further manifested the successful formation of the nanocomposite (Fig. 2d). Additional evidence was garnered by the corresponding energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ (Fig. S2), which detected the presence of typical elements including C, N, O, Zn and Eu. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction analysis in Fig. 2e confirmed the crystallinity and purity of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+. The distinct diffraction peaks of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ corresponded well with the established spectra of LZIF-8, verifying that the embedding of CDs as well as the grafting of Eu3+ did not destroy the crystal structure of LZIF-8, which aligns with the findings from electron microscopy. The specific surface area (S
BET ) of CDs@LZIF-8, measured at 1023.1 m2/g, is lower than that of LZIF-8, which measures 1573.6 m2/g (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the pore size distribution diagram of CDs@LZIF-8 indicates the absence of the aperture at 2 nm (Fig. S4). These observations further support the notion that CDs were effectively encapsulated within the pores of LcZIF-8. Additionally, the S
BET of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ decreased significantly to 88.3 m2/g, which can be attributed to the cross-linking of CitNa and the chelation with Eu3+ ions.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to identify the functional groups present in the composite materials at each stage of preparation. As displayed in Fig. 2f, the broad absorption band of CDs centered at 3414 cm−1 belongs to OH and NH groups. The stretching vibrations observed at 1709 cm−1, 1386 cm−1, and 1220 cm−1 confirm the retention of the oxygenated groups from citric acid and the nitrogenous groups from adenine during the hydrothermal reaction. The FT-IR absorption band at 3437 cm−1 in LZIF-8 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH bonds within imidazole rings. The peaks corresponding to NH and imidazole rings in CDs@LZIF-8 exhibit a noticeable redshift, suggesting that CDs may interact with these functional groups during the encapsulation process. Additionally, peaks related to the stretching vibrations of CN and ZnN bonds, along with the vibration peaks of the imidazole ring in LZIF-8, were detected at 1144/997 cm−1 and 1618/758/694 cm−1, respectively. Notably, the absorption peaks corresponding to the CDs and LZIF-8 all emerged in the FT-IR curve of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+, accompanied by slight shifts. These findings further demonstrate the successful synthesis of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+.

The XPS spectra of CDs, LZIF-8, CDs@LZIF-8 and CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ are presented in Fig.S5-S8. Following the encapsulation of CDs, the O 1 s spectrum of CDs@LZIF-8 shows a shift when compared to the spectra of both CDs and LZIF-8. In the XPS spectrum of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+, as illustrated in Fig. S8a, distinct binding energy peaks corresponding to the C1s, N1s, O1s, Zn2p, and Eu3d can be identified, which confirms the successful synthesis of the nanocomposites. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S8b) reveals three peaks at 284.6, 285.9, and 288.1 eV, which are associated with the binding energies of CC, C-N/C-O, and C=O/C=N, respectively. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S8c) can be fitted with three curves, showing peak values of 398.5, 399.5, and 400.6 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and NH. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S8d) can be broken down into two peaks at 530.75 and 532.6 eV, which relate to CO and CO. The Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. S8e) displays two peaks at 1021.7 and 1044.7 eV, attributed to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 (Li, Zhou, et al., 2019). In the Eu spectrum (Fig. S8f), the peaks at 1124.4, 1134.5, 1153.3, and 1164.4 eV correspond to Eu2+3d5/2, Eu3+2d5/2, Eu2+3d3/2, and Eu3+3d3/2, respectively. Furthermore, the energy disparity observed between two Eu3+ was approximately 29.8 eV, a phenomenon attributed to the coordination interactions between oxygen and Eu3+ (Shurygin et al., 2019).

The storage stability, photostability and thermostability of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ were assessed to evaluate their reliability and reproducibility in fluorescent performance. As shown in Fig. S9a, after the probes were stored at a temperature of 4 °C in a refrigerator for 30 days, a stable ratio of I617/I421 was maintained. Upon exposure to 365 nm irradiation for 24 h, the fluorescent properties of the probes remained essentially unchanged (Fig. S9b). Additionally, after exposure to heat treatments for 24 h at different temperatures (25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C) (Fig. S9c), the fluorescent properties of the probes were unaffected. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was also performed to evaluate the thermal stability of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ (Fig. S9d). The significant mass loss was observed at 80.3 °C, indicating a relatively good thermal stability of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and mechanism of the probe for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of the probes were investigated using UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs displayed two peaks at 260 nm and 335 nm while LZIF-8 did not display any absorption peaks. However, following the encapsulation of CDs, absorption peaks resembling those of CDs emerged in the CDs@LZIF-8 curve, indicating the successful penetration of CDs into the pores of LZIF-8. Upon coordination with Eu3+ to form CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+, the UV–visible absorption peak at 335 nm underwent a red shift, accompanied by a slight increase in absorption intensity. When TC was added, the absorption peak of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ sensing displayed a noticeable red shift. Additionally, the absorption peak intensity of the sensing system significantly rose compared to either TC or CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ alone, revealing the formation of a CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-TC complex through chelation of TC with Eu3+.

As shown in Fig. 3b, CDs demonstrate fluorescence peaks at 430 nm under the excitation of 335 nm. The fluorescence emission of the CDs@LZIF-8 composite is more intense than that of the original CDs under identical conditions. This increase in fluorescence is likely due to the confinement effect imposed by LZIF-8, which results in the uniform dispersion of CDs, overcomes the CDs agglomeration, and enhances their photoluminescence. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ composite is diminished compared to that of CDs@LZIF-8, suggesting energy transfer between the CDs and Eu3+, which can also be inferred from the partial overlap of the CDs emission spectrum and the LZIF-8-Eu3+ excitation spectrum in Fig. S10. Consequently, weak emission of Eu3+ (590–710 nm) can be found in the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ spectrum. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured at 6.17 %, whereas the QY of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ was comparatively lower, at 0.65 %. These results imply that CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ retains the unique fluorescence characteristics of CDs and Eu3+.

Upon the addition of TC, the fluorescence of the CDs decreased, while the characteristic emission of Eu3+ significantly enhanced. This enhancement indicates successful sensitization of the Eu3+ ions by the “antenna” molecule TC. Additionally, the fluorescence of the sensor exhibited an apparent color change from blue to red (see inset in Fig. 3b). Therefore, a reverse response sensor integrating CDs and Eu3+ via LZIF-8 was successfully constructed, with the fluorescence signal ratio (I617/I421) serving as a reliable metric for ratiometric sensing of TC.

It should be noted that the luminescence intensity of Eu3+ in the multi-component complexs LZIF-8-Eu3+-TC and CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-TC was five and ten times greater than that observed in the Eu3+-TC binary complex, respectively. This increase can be attributed to the presence of LZIF-8, which mitigates fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe due to the vibrational effects of water molecules on the Eu3+ coordination spheres, thereby enhancing fluorescence intensity. After the introduction of CDs, i.e. CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+, the energy exchange between CDs and Eu3+ further enhanced the emission of Eu3+. However, as shown in Fig. 3c, when there was no LZIF-8, that is, just simply mixing CDs and Eu3+, the emission of Eu3+ was absent after the addition of TC, and the fluorescence image only showed a monochromatic brightness change of CDs with the increase of TC concentration, implying the importance of Eu3+ combining with LZIF and the bridge role of LZIF-8 between CDs and Eu3+.

The adsorption capacity of LZIF-8 for TC has been also investigated. The specific adsorption experimental procedures are shown in Text S1 in the Supporting Information. As shown in Fig. S12, LZIF-8 has favorable adsorption characteristics to TC. By simply vibrating the mixtures of LZIF-8 and TC solution for a few seconds, the TC adsorption efficiency of LZIF-8 can be as high as 75.4 % at a concentration of 5 μg/mL, and even at a higher concentration of 30 μg/mL, the adsorption rate can still reach more than 40 %. The above results show that LZIF-8 not only has the role of linking CDs and Eu3+ but also has a rapid and efficient adsorption effect on the target object, which is conducive to increasing the probability of TC interacting with CDs and Eu3+.

To explore the potential mechanism underlying the fluorescence sensor for TC, a comprehensive investigation was conducted. Initially, to elucidate the fluorescence quenching process of CDs induced by TC, UV–visible absorption spectra of TC and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs were measured (refer to Fig. 3d). The UV–visible spectrum revealed absorption peaks of TC at 274 nm and 358 nm. Notably, the excitation spectrum of CDs exhibited partial overlap with the UV–visible absorption spectrum of TC, indicating the possible occurrence of the inner filter effect (IFE). Specifically, during IFE, the fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore remains constant, whereas it decreases in the case of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Cong et al., 2024; Song et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023). Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime was examined as an effective parameter to clarify the quenching mechanism. As depicted in Fig. S13, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was 3.75 ns, which changed to 3.63 ns when TC was introduced into the sensing system. The minimal change confirmed that the TC-induced fluorescence quenching was mainly attributable to IFE rather than FRET. Furthermore, quenching experiments were conducted and the fluorescence quenching process was analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)) (Priyadarshanee & Das, 2024; Ren, Labidi, et al., 2024):
(1)

F
0 and F denote the emission intensity of CDs before and after adding TC, respectively. Ksv (L·M−1) represents the quenching constant, C
q (M·L−1) represents the TC concentration, K
q (M−1·s−1) represents the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of CDs. The relationship between F/F
0 and C
q is shown in Fig. S14, with K
q calculated as 4.797 × 1012 M−1·s−1, significantly higher than that of dynamic quenching (1.0 × 1010 M−1·s−1). These findings suggest that TC-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs is due to the IFE and static quenching effect. In addition, the quenching efficiency due to the IFE was calculated to be 18.94 %, according to the previously reported method (Xin et al., 2022). The observed and corrected quenching efficiency of TC toward CDs is illustrated in Fig. S15. The detailed calculation information is presented in Supplementary Materials Text S2.

The mechanism behind the enhancement of Eu3+ fluorescence by TC was also investigated. Compared to free TC, the absorption peak of the Eu3+-TC binary system showed a significant redshift, from 358 nm to 382 nm, accompanied by an increase in absorption intensity, indicating the coordination of TC with Eu3+ (Fig. 3e). Additionally, the emission spectra of TC and the Eu3+-TC binary system were recorded (Fig. 3f), showing nearly complete quenching of TC emission after the addition of Eu3+ and an enhancement in the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ at 591 nm, 617 nm, and 697 nm. These observations suggest a highly effective energy transfer process from TC to Eu3+ ions. Specifically, the reported energy level of TC's T₁ state is 18,100 cm−1 (Jia et al., 2021), which is situated between the energies of Eu3+’s 5D₀ (17,260 cm−1) and 5D₁ (19,020 cm−1) levels, thereby facilitating energy transfer from TC to Eu3+. Consequently, the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ is observed, resulting from electronic transitions from 5D₀ to 7Fₗ (J = 1, 2, 3) (see Fig. S15).
To improve the detection performance of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+, several key experimental parameters including the ratio of CDs to Eu3+, pH values, and incubation time were systematically optimized.

The signal response of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ across a pH range of 3.0 to 11.0 is illustrated in Fig. S16. At pH 3.0, CDs exhibited a good response to TC, but the characteristic peak of Eu3+ was not detected under this condition. As the pH increased, the emission intensity of Eu3+ gradually rose, reaching a peak at pH 5.0–7.0, and then gradually decreased, while the response of CDs to TC remained relatively stable. One possible explanation is that, as the pH shifted from acidic to neutral conditions, TC transitioned from TC+ (cationic) to TC0 (molecule), which facilitated its binding into complexes with Eu3+, thereby enhancing the fluorescence emission. In an alkaline environment, TC converted to TC− (anion). The competition between OH− and TC− might weaken the coordination between TC and Eu3+, leading to a reduction in the antenna effect. As shown in Fig. S17f, the maximum value of I617/I421 was achieved at pH 7.0. Consequently, pH of 7.0 was selected as the optimal condition.

Next, to achieve the most visually appealing effect, the optimal fluorescence ratio (blue emission compared to red emission) was examined. Fig. S18 depicts the sensor responses at varying CDs to Eu3+ ratios, showcasing fluorescence spectra and color changes corresponding to different concentrations of TC. Notably, a noticeable transition in color from blue to red is evident in all cases. We analyzed the RGB values from the fluorescence color plots corresponding to various material ratios reacted with TC to construct a linear plot of the R/B against TC concentration. The findings indicated that a volume ratio of CDs to Eu3+ of 1:2 resulted in a higher linear slope, while further increases in the proportion of Eu3+ produced negligible changes in the slope (see Fig. S19). Thus, the ratio of CDs to Eu3+ at 1:2 was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Finally, by fixing the pH at 7.0 and maintaining a ratio of CDs to Eu3+ at 1:2, the reaction time was investigated (Fig. S20). The addition of TC resulted in the I617/I421 signal reaching near equilibrium within 10 s and maintaining stability for up to 10 min. This observation indicates the formation of a highly stable complex CDs@ZIF-8-Eu3+-TC.

Accurate detection of targets in complex samples necessitates analytical methods that exhibit specificity and anti-interference capabilities. The interaction of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ with TCs may be impeded by the presence of metal ions and various organic compounds in real samples. Therefore, the response of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ sensor to a variety of potential interfering substances in environment and food samples was evaluated, including glucose, sucrose, representative amino acids (glycine, tryptophan, lysine), common inorganic ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Fe3+), antibiotics (including ciprofloxacin, difloxacin, ofloxacin, sarafloxacin, ornidazole, tinidazole, sulfisoxazole, sulfamethoxypyridazine, sulfachlorpyridazine), and fungicide thiophanate. As shown in Fig. S21 and Fig. S22, under the same experimental conditions, the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ sensor selectively responded to only four TCs—namely, TC and its analogs (oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, and doxycycline)—to varying extents, without interference from other potential distractors. These findings indicate that the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ sensor demonstrates exceptional specificity in monitoring TCs. The notable selectivity and anti-interference capabilities of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ arise from the specific response of CDs and Eu3+ to TCs. Additionally, the inherent calibration effect of these two light sources can also significantly enhance the probe's anti-interference performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of TCs based on CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para: To align with the actual application, the sensor's performance toward different levels (low, medium, and high concentrations) of TCs was investigated under optimal conditions using real blank water (tap water). The absence of TCs in the sample was confirmed by HPLC-MS/MS (Fig. S23). As depicted in Fig. 4a, before the introduction of TC, prominent CDs fluorescence with no discernible emission from Eu3+ was observed. Upon the addition of TC, and with the increasing concentration, the characteristic emission peak of Eu3+ showed a gradual enhancement, while the emission peak of CDs displayed a consistent decrease along with a certain degree of red-shift. A satisfactory linear correlation was established between the intensity ratio I617/I421 of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ and TC concentration within the concentration range of 0.01 to 30 μg/mL, represented by the equation I617/I421 = 0.24CTC + 0.17, which yielded a high correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.994 (see Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 3.11 ng/mL (7.0 nM) based on the standard 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the signal measured from the blank sample, and k is the slope of the calibration curve. Notably, the LOD achieved is significantly lower than the maximum permissible concentrations set by regulatory agencies, including the US FDA, the EU, and China (676 nM, 225 nM, and 225 nM, respectively) (Ran et al., 2024). The recoveries of TC ranged from 92.0 % to 106.8 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) between 1.48 % and 4.35 %, as detailed in Table S1. Inter-day precision was assessed by conducting duplicate analyses at each concentration level over three consecutive days. According to the results presented in Table S2, satisfactory inter-day precision was achieved, with RSDs below 8.1 %. These findings highlight the potential of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ for practical applications in real samples. Additionally, under a 365 nm UV lamp, the fluorescence color of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ probe exhibited successive changes from blue to purple, and eventually to red as TC concentrations increased, which corresponded with CIE chromaticity diagrams (Fig. 4c).

The fluorescence response of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ to other three TCs, namely OTC, DOX and CTC, were similar with TC, resulting in a significant enhancement of I617/I421 signals, as shown in Fig. 4d. Likewise, a satisfactory linear relationship was achieved, with limits of detection (LODs) determined to be 2.94 ng/mL (6.4 nM) for OTC, 3.78 ng/mL (15.0 nM) FOR DOX, and 4.46 ng/mL (9.3 nM) for CTC, respectively. Furthermore, continuous fluorescence color changes of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ probe from blue to red with increasing TCs concentrations were clearly observable, as shown in Fig. S24–26.

For comparison, CDs@LZIF-8 and LZIF-8-Eu3+ were employed as single-signal fluorescence sensors for the quantitative analysis of TC under the same detection procedures, as recorded in Fig. 4e-h. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@LZIF-8 and LZIF-8-Eu3+ at 445 nm and 620 nm exhibited good linear responses to TC in the range of 0.5–30 μg/mL and 0.05–30 μg/mL, respectively, with LODs of 0.141 μg/mL and 0.023 μg/mL. The linear equations are I445 = 9027.4CTC + 510,500.9 (R2 = 0.991) and I620 = 8979.8CTC + 22,526.0 (R2 = 0.971). Apparently, the single-signal fluorescence sensors based on CDs@LZIF-8 and LZIF-8-Eu3+ exhibited lower correlation coefficients and higher LODs compared to the dual-channel fluorescence sensor. This disparity may be attributed to the inherent self-calibration feature of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor. More importantly, compared with the noticeable color changes of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+, the single-channel detection system demonstrated only a monochromatic brightness alteration in response to varying TC concentrations. These findings strongly support that the ratiometric fluorescent sensor (CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+) provides distinct advantages over the single-emission sensors (CDs@LZIF-8 and LZIF-8-Eu3+).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cross-validation test between the HPLC-MS/MS and developed CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-based fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the accuracy and reliability of the quantitative results of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+, we conducted a cross-validation test. Liquid chromatography is the most common used method for detecting TCs in environmental and food samples. Therefore, we used the HPLC-MS/MS alongside the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-based ratiometric fluorescent platform to measure TCs at a concentration of 50 ng/mL in various water samples including lake water, tap water and reservoir water, as detailed in Section 2.4. Before conducting spike experiments, we confirmed the absence of TC-like antibiotics through HPLC-MS/MS analysis (refer to Fig. S27). Subsequently, standard working solutions of TC were added to the prepared samples, and the concentrations of the target compounds were measured using both HPLC-MS/MS and fluorescence spectrometer. The TC concentration detected by HPLC-MS/MS ranged from 43.9 to 49.8 ng/mL, with an error margin of 0.4 % to 12.2 % (Fig. S28). In contrast, the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-based platform yielded results ranging from 47.2 to 50.6 ng/mL, with an error margin of 0.2 % to 5.6 % (Fig. S29). Detailed analysis procedures and parameters of HPLC-MS/MS are provided in the Supporting Information (Text S3 and Text S4). The Bland-Altman plot was utilized to evaluate the potential discrepancies between the HPLC-MS/MS and CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-based methods. As illustrated in Fig. 4i, most data points fell within ±1.96 standard deviations of the mean difference (95 % confidence interval), indicating a satisfactory agreement between the two techniques.

Furthermore, a more comprehensive comparison of the two methods is presented in Fig. 5 . The developed CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ based fluorescence detection method significantly streamlines the process, requiring only a single operational step. This method also utilizes a minimal sample volume of just 1.8 mL and does not require organic solvents. Moreover, it offers a markedly shorter detection time of only one minute. In contrast, the conventional HPLC-MS/MS detection method can take up to one hour to complete and incurs higher costs due to the multiple components involved (e.g., reagents, pumps, columns, and mass spectrometer, etc). Thus, the developed method is rapid, simple, and cost-effective. Additionally, compared to the laboratory-based setup of HPLC-MS/MS, the portability of the sensor makes it more suitable for on-site screening applications.

To evaluate the versatility of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ platform for various scenarios, the TC with various concentrations in multiple blank food samples, including chicken meat, eggs, milk, and honey, all of which showed no detectable levels of TC using HPLC-MS/MS. The specific experimental procedures are detailed in Text S5. As shown in Fig. S30, CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ exhibited a good linear response to TC in the range of 20 ng/g-2.5 μg/g with LODs of 4.87 ng/g, 6.49 ng/g, 4.97 ng/g, 5.59 ng/g in chicken meat, egg, milk and honey samples, respectively. The recoveries of different TC concentrations ranged from 88.7 % to 113.2 %, with RSDs between 0.30 % and 5.79 % (Table S1). These results confirm the strong recognition capability of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor, demonstrating its suitability for real sample applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with other fluorescence detection methods for TCs
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the effectiveness of the method, the performance of the proposed analytical technique was compared with other previously reported fluorescence detection methods for TCs (Bai et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2023; Cui et al., 2022; Hui et al., 2024; Li, Tian, et al., 2024; Li, Zhao, et al., 2018; Li, Zhu, et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2021; Venugopalan & Vidya, 2023; Wei et al., 2019; Xin et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022; Yuan et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2025; Zhang, Ma, et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2018). As shown in Table 1 , many existing probes for TCs exhibit single fluorescence signal changes and generally have high LODs. Furthermore, the LZIF-8 in the composite material has been demonstrated to enhance the response of Eu3+ to TC, so as to obtain a more obvious chromaticity change after reaction with the target object. Notably, our probe exhibits a relatively low detection limit compared to other probes, and its response time is significantly shorter than that of other sensors. Additionally, the first solid-phase fluorescence sensing platform based on in situ growth-prepared nanofiber mat has been developed for the detection of TCs. Moreover, compared to other methods, our proposed approach can be applied to a wide range of samples, including water, chicken meat, eggs, milk, and honey. LZIF-8 in this work not only serves as a supporter for CDs and Eu3+, but also exhibits effective adsorption properties that facilitates both enrichment and purification of the target substances. This functionality is conducive to enhancing the sensing properties of the material, enabling its application across various practical samples.

The accurate differentiation of multiple tetracyclines (TCs) in fluorescence analysis presents a significant challenge. Principal Component Analysis (PCA), a crucial tool in spectroscopy analysis, is commonly used to reduce the dimensionality of datasets, thereby facilitating the identification of substances with similar structures and properties. Variations in substituent groups (Fig. S31) lead to differing affinities for Eu3+, causing the four types of TCs to exhibit distinct sensitizing abilities toward CDs and Eu3+, resulting in varying fluorescence intensities and differing R/B values. To make it easier to observe, we made a heatmap of the differences in the RGB value responses of different TCs (Fig. S32). By utilizing these variable parameters, we attempt to distinguish TCs using PCA. As depicted in Fig. S33a-d, the sensor successfully differentiated between different concentrations (1, 5, 15, and 30 μg/mL) of TCs, where the signal points for each concentration clearly clustered into distinct groups. Furthermore, an examination was conducted to assess the sensor's ability to distinguish among different TCs within the same system, as shown in Fig. S33e-h. The results indicated that the four TCs, each at different concentrations, were spread across sixteen unique and non-overlapping regions. Additionally, PC1 showed a good relationship with the concentration of TCs (Fig. S34). The mixtures of two types of TCs, both at identical and different concentrations could also be distinguished, as illustrated in Fig. S33i-j. When three types of TCS were mixed, the signal points for each type clustered into distinct groups, but there was a slight crossover. (Fig. S33k) This indicates that the sensor has a comparable ability to qualitatively and quantitatively distinguish among four types of TCs. Since TCs can be chelated with a variety of lanthanide ions and have different degrees of fluorescence enhancement for different TCs targets, it is possible to construct sensor arrays by grafting different lanthanide ions, such as Tb3+, Sm3+, and Dy3+, to enhance the quantization ability for diverse TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted portable lab-on-nanofibers as a sensing platform for real-time monitoring of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Although the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ possessed excellent sensitivity and selectivity for TCs detection, it was impractical for on-site analysis due to the requirement of bulky instruments. Furthermore, visual analysis relying on the naked eye was hindered by the challenge of discerning subtle color variations. To overcome these limitations and enable accurate on-site and real-time TCs analysis, a portable Lab-on-Nanofibers sensing platform was developed, which consists of a custom smartphone-assisted device and CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-based nanofibers mat.

SEM images of the nanofibers before and after modification (Fig. 6a-b) reveal that the surface of the fibers becomes rough and is covered with small protrusions following modification. Notably, numerous evenly distributed pores approximately 10–20 nm in diameter are visible on the nanofiber surface, possibly formed during the growth of LZIF-8. The typical morphology of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+-based nanofibers mat facilitates the intra-mat diffusion of the target, leading to the dispersion of TC across the surface of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ NFM and its infiltration into the inner layers, thus improving sensing capabilities of the material.

The self-assembled apparatus comprises a black box, a 365 nm UV light source, and a smartphone. The configuration of the black box is illustrated in Fig. S35. This device not only provides a dark operational environment and a light source with a specific excitation wavelength but also determines the distance from the samples to the light source and smartphone, thereby enhancing the practicality and precision of the detection process. The upper section of the box is equipped with a shooting hole for photographing the mat. The nanofiber mat was segmented into strips, soaked in various concentrations of TC solutions, and incubated for 10 s. Subsequently, the nanofiber mat was extracted, dried, and positioned in the designated sample slot. Following this, the smartphone was employed to capture fluorescence images under a 365 nm UV light. Simultaneously, the color images were digitized and converted into RGB values using the Color Picker application installed on the smartphone (refer to Fig. 6c). As depicted in Fig. 6d, the nanofiber mat exhibited a color transition from blue to red with increasing TC concentration. Furthermore, the R/B values displayed a strong linear correlation with TC concentration within the range of 0.02 to 30 μg/mL, with the corresponding linear equation represented as R/B = 0.0329× + 0.4845 (R2 = 0.977) (see Fig. 6e). Additionally, a low detection limit of 4.6 ng/mL was achieved through the 3σ/k.

To evaluate the uniformity of the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanofiber mat, inter-batch assays were conducted using three consecutively prepared batches of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanofiber mat, each tested with varying concentrations of TC solutions (1, 5, and 20 μg/mL). The inter-batch variation was assessed by comparing the measured TC concentration values of each batch (a, b, and c) using one-way ANOVA and Tukey's test. As shown in Fig. S36, the differences in TC concentrations within each group were not statistically significant, indicating that the preparation procedure of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanofiber mat provided a good batch-to-batch reproducibility.

The stability of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ has also been investigated. As shown in Fig. S37, after being stored for 0, 1, 3, 7, 15, and 30 days, the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanofibers mat showed a stable ratio of R/G. In addition, after the reaction of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ with TC, the colorimetric channels R and G still maintained a stable ratio even after 30 days, allowing long-term visualization in practical scenarios.

The visual performance of the proposed Lab-on-Nanofibers sensing platform was compared with other solid phase fluorescence sensing methods for TCs in the literature (Chen et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2024; Hu et al., 2022; Jia, Chen, et al., 2022; Jia, Xu, et al., 2022; Jian et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2022), as presented in Table 2 . Carriers such as filter paper or film possess small surface areas that restrict internal mass transfer. Consequently, fluorescent probes can only be loaded onto the surfaces of these carriers at low load rates and has limited interaction with the target. Furthermore, methods such as dipping or doping do not ensure stable probe loading, which compromises sensing performance. The CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanofiber mat developed in this study represents the first solid-phase sensing platform prepared using an in-situ growth method, achieving a sufficiently stable probe load. Additionally, the CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ was designed based on a model in which one signal is enhanced while the other is diminished. This design, combined with a broad emission gamut ranging from blue to red, facilitates its significant visual signal changes even at low concentrations of the target substances. Notably, the Lab-on-Nanofibers developed in this work demonstrated a lower visual limit of detection (LOD) compared to many previously reported methods. Moreover, the sensor's response time is rapid, requiring only 10 s. These results indicate that the proposed portable smartphone-assisted platform has significant practical applications for the rapid on-site screening of TCs.

In summary, an ingenious ratiometric sensing nanoprobe based on LZIF-8 was successfully developed. The LZIF-8 as supporter not only boosts the fluorescence of CDs through a confinement effect but also shields Eu3+ from the OH vibrational of H2O molecules, thus enhancing the fluorescence of Eu3+ when it coordinates with TCs. Additionally, LZIF-8 serves as an adsorbent for target objects. Benefiting from the versatile LZIF-8 and an efficient dual-channel strategy, the sensor demonstrates a rapid response time (10 s), high selectivity and sensitivity toward TC, OTC, CTC and DOX, with LODs of 3.11, 2.94, 4.46 and 3.78 ng/mL, respectively. The sensor also exhibits a distinct fluorescence color change from blue to red. Moreover, it has been successfully applied to detect TCs in environmental and food samples, yielding satisfactory recoveries and precisions. Compared to the established HPLC-MS/MS method, the newly developed sensing platform has high reliability for the detection of TC in actual water environment and has shown to be straightforward, rapid, and environmentally friendly. The sensor enabled simultaneous detection of tetracycline analogs qualitatively and quantitatively by PCA. The wide-gamut fluorescence change was beneficial to human eye recognition and the first portable nanofibers mat-based solid-phase fluorescence sensing platform was fabricated by in-situ growth method. Combined with a smartphone-assisted device, rapid and sensitive on-site monitoring of TCs can be achieved. The high stability, high sensitivity, and rapid responses of CDs@LZIF-8-Eu3+ nanofiber mat underscore its practical feasibility for on-site target screening. This study not only presents a novel approach for designing fluorescence probes but also introduces a promising visual method based on nanofiber mat for convenient and real-time monitoring.

Yueyi Shen: Formal analysis. Li-e Liu: Writing – review & editing. Tong Li: Validation. Ruili Liu: Supervision. Jingyi Zhang: Software. Jiaojiao Chen: Visualization. Xuqing Zhang: Data curation. Xinyi Li: Resources, Methodology. Ningge Jian: Writing – original draft. Di Wu: Funding acquisition, Resources.
